text: Concurrent strength and endurance training significantly increases knee flexion (hamstring) strength more than endurance training alone in distance runners.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: Concurrent strength and endurance training significantly increases ankle plantarflexion (calf) strength more than endurance training alone in distance runners.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: Concurrent strength and endurance training significantly increases squat strength more than endurance training alone in distance runners.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: Concurrent strength and endurance training significantly increases knee extension (quadriceps) strength more than endurance training alone in distance runners, though with moderate evidence.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: Concurrent strength and endurance training does not significantly improve jump height compared to endurance training alone in distance runners.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: Concurrent strength and endurance training improves the force-generating capacity of ankle plantarflexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, and gluteal muscles, which support and propel the centre of mass and accelerate the leg during running.
population: distance runners of mixed training status

text: There is currently no evidence that strength gains from concurrent training transfer directly to changes in biomechanical or neuromuscular variables during actual running.
population: distance runners of mixed training status
